section.title: The Six Seals
section.sectionRationale: Having established in chapter 5 that the Lamb alone is worthy to open the scroll, John now shows the Lamb actually breaking its seals one by one. The first four seals form a tight unit of four horsemen, the fifth and sixth are each longer and distinct, and the seventh seal is deliberately delayed until 8:1, with chapter 7 forming an interlude between the sixth and seventh. This chapter is therefore its own coherent movement, the unleashing of six seals, ending on the terrified cry 'who is able to withstand it,' a question chapter 7 will answer.
section.thesis: As the Lamb breaks seal after seal, conquest, war, famine, and death ride out under God's sovereign permission, martyred souls cry out for justice from under the altar, and the created order itself convulses, so that every earthly power is left with nowhere to hide from the wrath of the One on the throne and of the Lamb.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the first seal, a rider bent on conquest; the second, third, and fourth seals, war, scarcity, and death riding together; the fifth seal, the martyrs' cry from under the altar; and the sixth seal, cosmic upheaval and universal terror before the wrath of the Lamb.

verses.text: Then I watched as the Lamb opened one of the seven seals, and I heard one of the four living creatures say in a thunderous voice, “Come!”
So I looked and saw a white horse, and its rider held a bow. And he was given a crown, and he rode out to {{nikao|overcome}} and conquer.

units.label: The second, third, and fourth seals

verses.text: And when the Lamb opened the second seal, I heard the second living creature say, “Come!”
Then another horse went forth. It was bright red, and its rider was granted permission to take away peace from the earth and to make men slay one another. And he was given a great sword.
And when the Lamb opened the third seal, I heard the third living creature say, “Come!” Then I looked and saw a black horse, and its rider held in his hand a pair of scales.

And I heard what sounded like a voice from among the four living creatures, saying, “A quart of wheat for a {{denarion|denarius}}, and three quarts of barley for a denarius, and do not harm the oil and wine.”
And when the Lamb opened the fourth seal, I heard the voice of the fourth living creature say, “Come!”

Then I looked and saw a pale green horse. Its rider's name was Death, and Hades followed close behind. And they were given authority over a fourth of the earth, to kill by sword, by famine, by plague, and by the beasts of the earth.
And when the Lamb opened the fifth seal, I saw under the altar the {{psyche|souls}} of those who had been slain for the word of God and for the testimony they had upheld.
And they cried out in a loud voice, “How long, O Lord, holy and true, until You avenge our blood and judge those who dwell upon the earth?”
Then each of them was given a white robe and told to rest a little while longer, until the full number of their fellow servants, their brothers, were killed, just as they had been killed.
And when I saw the Lamb open the sixth seal, there was a great earthquake, and the sun became black like sackcloth of goat hair, and the whole moon turned blood red,
and the stars of the sky fell to the earth like unripe figs dropping from a tree shaken by a great wind.
The sky receded like a scroll being rolled up, and every mountain and island was moved from its place.
Then the kings of the earth, the nobles, the commanders, the rich, the mighty, and every slave and free man hid in the caves and among the rocks of the mountains.
And they said to the mountains and the rocks, “Fall on us and hide us from the face of the One seated on the throne, and from the {{orge|wrath}} of the Lamb.
For the great day of Their wrath has come, and who is able to withstand it?”

nikao.gloss: To conquer, to overcome, to be victorious. The first rider goes out already 'overcoming' and 'to conquer,' a doubled use of the same root that stresses relentless, ongoing military triumph. Revelation elsewhere reserves this verb for Christ and the overcoming saints, making its use here for a rider of judgment a deliberately unsettling irony.

denarion.gloss: A denarius, a Roman silver coin equal to a laborer's typical day's wage. That a whole day's pay would buy only a single day's ration of wheat, or barely three days of barley, pictures severe famine-level inflation, while oil and wine, the crop of deep-rooted trees and vines, are spared, showing scarcity held within limits God still sets.

psyche.gloss: Soul, life, the seat of a person's being. The souls under the altar are the martyrs whose lifeblood, poured out for their faithfulness, is pictured as a sacrifice at the base of the heavenly altar. They are consciously alive in God's presence, awaiting the completion of their number and the vindication their cry asks for.

orge.gloss: Wrath, settled and just anger, distinct from a sudden fit of rage. Here it is startlingly called the wrath 'of the Lamb,' fusing the image of gentle sacrifice with righteous judgment; the same Lamb who was slain for sinners is also the one whose wrath the unrepentant cannot stand before.

basic.context: The Lamb begins breaking the scroll's seven seals, and each breaking releases a vision. Four horsemen ride out in quick succession: conquest, war, scarcity, and death. Then the scene shifts to heaven, where martyred believers cry from under an altar, asking how long until God judges their killers; they are told to wait a little longer. Finally the sixth seal brings a cosmic shaking, sun and moon darkened, stars falling, mountains moved, and every class of person on earth, from kings to slaves, hiding in terror from the wrath of the One on the throne and of the Lamb.
basic.hermeneutics: Readers have long debated whether these seals describe events already fulfilled in the first century, patterns recurring throughout church history, a specific future tribulation, or timeless symbols of the kind of upheaval that marks every age until Christ returns. Wise interpreters hold these views with humility, since faithful readers disagree, but every view agrees on the chapter's central claim: history's calamities unfold only because the Lamb permits them, and God's people are never abandoned in the midst of them.
basic.originalLanguages: The first rider goes out nikao, conquering and to conquer, doubling the verb of victory that Revelation elsewhere reserves for Christ and His people, an unsettling echo. The third seal's denarion, a day's wage, buys barely a day's food, picturing severe scarcity. The martyrs are psyche, souls, consciously alive under the altar, crying for justice. And the terrified world hides from the orge, wrath, of the Lamb, the same Lamb once slain now revealed as the one whose judgment cannot be escaped.
basic.typology: The four horsemen echo the ancient covenant curses of sword, famine, plague, and wild beasts named in Israel's law. The martyrs' cry from under the altar recalls Abel's blood crying out from the ground in Genesis. And the cosmic darkening of sun, moon, and stars fulfills prophetic pictures of the Day of the LORD found throughout the Old Testament prophets, now applied to the climactic day of the Lamb's wrath.
basic.christ: It is the Lamb, and only the Lamb, who breaks each seal; every horseman rides, every martyr's cry is heard, and every cosmic convulsion happens strictly because He permits it. The chapter that began with His worthiness in chapter 5 continues here with His sovereignty over history's darkest hours, and it ends with the world recognizing, in terror rather than faith, that even the wrath they face is His.
basic.god: God governs even the world's worst violence and disorder; nothing here is outside His control, since it is the Lamb who authorizes each seal's release. Yet God is not indifferent to the suffering of His people; He hears the martyrs' cry and answers it, not by ignoring justice but by setting His own perfect timing for it, asking them to wait until the full number of the faithful has been gathered.
basic.crossReferences: Zechariah 1 and 6 describe four colored horses sent to patrol and report on the earth, an image John reshapes here as active agents of judgment. Ezekiel 14:21 names sword, famine, wild beasts, and plague together as God's four sore judgments. Joel 2:31 and other prophets describe the sun darkened and moon turned to blood before the great Day of the LORD, language this chapter directly echoes.
basic.oneStory: From Eden onward the Bible has warned that a world in rebellion against God will reap conquest, war, scarcity, and death, yet has also promised that God hears the cry of His suffering people. Here that whole story converges: the Lamb who was slain now sovereignly permits the very calamities the prophets foretold, martyrs are comforted rather than ignored, and even the mightiest of the earth are brought low before the wrath of the Lamb, driving every reader to ask which side of that day they stand on.

context.literary: This chapter follows directly from chapter 5's establishment of the Lamb's worthiness; here that worthiness is put into action as He breaks seal after seal. The first four seals form a matched set of four horsemen, the fifth and sixth are longer and more developed, and the seventh seal is deliberately withheld until 8:1, with all of chapter 7 forming a comforting interlude between the sixth and seventh seals.
context.historical: Early readers under Roman rule would have recognized real anxieties in these images: the threat of invading cavalry, the civil wars that plagued the empire in the years surrounding its writing, grain shortages and price inflation, and disease. The vision does not merely predict distant events; it names realities its first hearers were already living through, and interprets them within God's sovereign purposes.
context.canonical: This chapter draws on Zechariah's four horses, Ezekiel's four judgments, and the prophets' Day of the LORD language, gathering threads from across the Old Testament into a single unfolding vision, one that itself becomes the template later echoed in the trumpet and bowl judgments of Revelation's later chapters.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the risen, worthy Lamb is sovereign over history's worst upheavals, that He hears and will vindicate His suffering people, and that no earthly power, however great, can withstand His wrath, calling every reader to repentance and to patient endurance.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a series of visionary judgments; it does not prescribe specific historical dates or events to be matched one for one against current news. Its abiding truths are God's sovereignty over calamity, His care for the martyred, and the certainty of final judgment.

debates.question: What do the six seals represent, and are they past, present, or future events?

views.label: Preterist: judgments tied to the first century
views.case: Many of the earliest readers lived through the civil wars of AD 68 to 70, famine, and the catastrophic Jewish revolt against Rome; on this view the seals describe, in apocalyptic imagery, judgments that fell within a generation of the book's writing, particularly surrounding the fall of Jerusalem, vindicating persecuted believers who saw these events unfold.

views.label: Historicist: judgments unfolding across church history
views.case: Historic interpreters, especially from the Reformation era onward, read the seals as a symbolic sketch of successive periods of church and world history, invasions, wars, and calamities recurring across the centuries between Christ's first and second comings, each seal corresponding to an identifiable historical epoch.

views.label: Futurist: judgments belonging to a coming tribulation
views.case: Many modern interpreters read the seals as still future, describing a specific, intensified period of tribulation shortly before Christ's return, when conquest, war, famine, and death will strike the world with a severity beyond anything yet seen, fulfilling Jesus' own Olivet Discourse predictions of the 'beginning of birth pains.'

views.label: Idealist: timeless patterns of the church age
views.case: Others read the seals as symbolic of the recurring character of history itself, war, injustice, scarcity, and death that mark every generation between the cross and the return of Christ, not tied to one century or one future moment but describing the ongoing 'birth pains' the church endures throughout this present age.

debates.bearing: Each school captures something true: real judgment fell in the first century, history has indeed unfolded in recurring waves of upheaval, a future intensification before Christ's return is entirely possible, and every generation experiences some version of these four horsemen. The passage's pastoral force does not depend on settling the timing precisely; it depends on trusting that the Lamb, not chance or chaos, sovereignly holds the reins in every age.

debates.question: Who or what does the white horse and its rider in the first seal represent?

views.label: Christ or the triumphant spread of the gospel
views.case: A white horse later appears in Revelation 19 with Christ Himself as its rider, and the crown given to this first rider could suggest a picture of Christ's own conquest through the gospel going out to the nations, consistent with Jesus' words that the gospel must first be preached to all nations.

views.label: A symbol of military conquest, not Christ
views.case: This rider appears as one of a matched set of four, grouped with war, famine, and death, all clearly judgments rather than blessings, and the crown given here is a lesser crown of ordinary victory, not the many diadems worn by Christ in chapter 19; on this reading the white horse pictures the ambition of empire and conquest itself, a counterfeit of Christ's true triumph.

debates.bearing: Most interpreters favor the second view because of the horse's placement among three other clearly judgment-bearing riders, but the debate itself illustrates how carefully Revelation's imagery must be read in its immediate context, since the same symbol can carry different meanings in different scenes of the book.

study.originalLanguages: The first rider goes out already nikao, conquering, and continues 'to conquer,' the same verb Revelation elsewhere reserves for Christ's own victory and for the overcoming saints, a jarring echo that hints how conquest without the Lamb's character becomes mere destruction. The third seal's denarion, a laborer's full day's wage, buying only a single day's grain, pictures famine pressing hard on the poor while oil and wine, drawn from deep roots, are mercifully spared. The martyrs beneath the altar are called psyche, souls, a term implying conscious, ongoing life in God's presence even after death, crying out for the orge, wrath, that finally arrives in the sixth seal, wrath now startlingly attributed to the Lamb Himself, the one once slain now revealed as the one whose judgment none can withstand.

typology.type: The four covenant judgments, sword, famine, plague, and wild beasts, Leviticus 26 and Ezekiel 14:21
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled and intensified in the four horsemen, showing that the covenant curses warned of long ago are enacted at the Lamb's command.

typology.type: Abel's blood crying out from the ground, Genesis 4:10
typology.fulfillment: Echoed in the martyrs' cry from under the altar, the first instance of innocent blood demanding justice fulfilled in the final vindication of all the faithful slain.

typology.type: The darkened sun, moon, and stars of the Day of the LORD, Joel 2:31 and Isaiah 13:10
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the sixth seal's cosmic shaking, the prophetic sign now enacted as the Lamb's own day of wrath draws near.

study.christ: It is the Lamb who breaks every seal in this chapter; nothing here proceeds apart from His direct action. The same Lamb whose worthiness rested on being slain in chapter 5 is here shown sovereign over conquest, war, scarcity, and death, hearing the cry of His martyred people and, by the sixth seal, revealed as the one whose wrath the mightiest kings of the earth cannot withstand. The chapter refuses to let readers separate the gentle Lamb of Calvary from the sovereign judge of history; they are one and the same, and history unfolds only within the scroll He alone was worthy to open.
study.god: God governs history's worst violence without ever losing control of it; every rider, every judgment, every earthquake proceeds because the Lamb permits it, not because evil has slipped its leash. Yet God is never indifferent to suffering; He receives the martyrs' cry as a real prayer deserving a real answer, and He sets His own perfect timing for justice, asking His people to trust Him for 'a little while longer.' His wrath, described here as belonging jointly to Him and the Lamb, is not arbitrary cruelty but the necessary, righteous response to a world that has rejected Him.

crossReferences.note: Four colored horses patrol the earth as God's agents, imagery John reshapes into active instruments of judgment in this chapter.
Names sword, famine, wild beasts, and plague as God's four severe judgments, echoed directly in the fourth seal.
The cry to the mountains, 'fall on us,' quoted almost word for word in verse 16, roots this scene in an ancient prophetic warning of judgment.
Foretells the sun darkened and moon turned to blood before the great Day of the LORD, fulfilled in the sixth seal's cosmic upheaval.

study.oneStory: Since the fall, the Bible has warned that a world at war with God reaps conquest, bloodshed, scarcity, and death, and has promised that the blood of the innocent will not go unanswered forever. This chapter shows that whole story converging under the authority of the slain Lamb, who alone releases these judgments, who hears His martyred people's cry for justice, and before whose wrath, on the last day, every human power will be left with nowhere to hide, save in the mercy of the very Lamb they once refused.

sermon.title: The Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse
sermon.why: Carson preaches directly through Revelation 6, working seal by seal from the four horsemen through the martyrs' cry to the sixth seal's cosmic terror, and closes by pointing to the slain Lamb as the only refuge from the very wrath this chapter describes, engaging the full sweep of this section.

quiz.q: Who breaks each of the six seals in this chapter?

options.text: The Lamb, established in chapter 5 as alone worthy to open the scroll.
options.note: Every seal in this chapter is explicitly broken by the Lamb, verses 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 12.

options.text: One of the twenty-four elders, acting on the Lamb's behalf.
options.note: The elders worship throughout Revelation, but it is the Lamb Himself, not an elder, who breaks each seal.

options.text: The four living creatures, who summon the horsemen without the Lamb's involvement.
options.note: The living creatures call out 'Come' after each seal is opened, but it is the Lamb who does the opening.

options.text: An angel sent from the altar of incense.
options.note: The altar and its angel appear later, in chapter 8; the seals in this chapter are broken by the Lamb alone.

quiz.q: What do the second, third, and fourth seals release upon the earth?

options.text: War and bloodshed, severe famine-level scarcity, and death by sword, famine, plague, and wild beasts.
options.note: Verses 3-8 describe exactly these three riders and their effects.

options.text: Only peace and abundance across the whole earth.
options.note: The opposite; peace is taken away and scarcity, not abundance, follows.

options.text: A single plague of locusts covering a third of the earth.
options.note: Locusts appear later, in the fifth trumpet of chapter 9, not among these three seals.

options.text: Total, immediate destruction of the entire earth with no survivors.
options.note: The fourth seal explicitly limits its authority to a fourth of the earth; the judgment is severe but bounded.

quiz.q: What do the martyred souls under the altar cry out for in the fifth seal, and what are they told?

options.text: They cry, 'How long until You judge and avenge our blood,' and are told to rest a little longer until the full number of martyrs is complete.
options.note: Verses 9-11 record this exact cry and the answer given to them.

options.text: They cry for mercy on their persecutors and are granted it immediately.
options.note: Their cry is for justice and vindication, not for mercy on their persecutors, and the answer is to wait, not immediate action.

options.text: They ask to be sent back to earth to finish their mission.
options.note: Their request concerns God's judgment and vindication, not a return to earthly mission.

options.text: They are silenced and told never to speak again.
options.note: They are given white robes and told to rest, not silenced; their cry is heard and answered with a promise.

quiz.q: What happens when the Lamb opens the sixth seal?

options.text: A great earthquake, the sun and moon darkened, stars falling, and every class of person hiding in terror from the wrath of the Lamb.
options.note: Verses 12-17 describe exactly this cosmic upheaval and universal terror.

options.text: A season of complete peace descends on the earth.
options.note: The opposite occurs; the sixth seal brings terrifying cosmic disturbance, not peace.

options.text: Only the wicked kings of the earth are affected, while everyone else remains untouched.
options.note: The text names kings, nobles, commanders, the rich, the mighty, slave, and free, a comprehensive list, not kings alone.

options.text: The scroll is fully opened and history reaches its final end.
options.note: The scroll's full opening awaits the seventh seal in chapter 8; the sixth seal itself brings terror, not the final consummation.

quiz.q: What is the significance of describing the coming judgment as 'the wrath of the Lamb' in verse 16?

options.text: It shows the same Lamb once slain for sinners is also the sovereign judge whose wrath none can withstand.
options.note: This paradox is central to the chapter's climax and to Revelation's larger portrait of Christ.

options.text: It means the wrath belongs only to angels, not to Christ Himself.
options.note: The text specifically names the wrath as belonging to the Lamb and to the One on the throne, not to angels.

options.text: It shows that lambs are naturally fierce and dangerous animals.
options.note: The force of the phrase is theological paradox, sacrificial gentleness paired with righteous judgment, not a comment on animal behavior.

options.text: It indicates the wrath has already ended by this point in the vision.
options.note: The wrath is described as just arriving, 'the great day of their wrath has come,' not as already past.

questions: The four horsemen ride out only because the Lamb permits it. When the news feels like conquest, war, scarcity, and death are winning, how does remembering the Lamb's sovereignty change the way you carry that weight?
The martyrs under the altar were told to wait 'a little while longer.' Where in your own life do you need to trust God's timing for justice rather than demanding it on your own schedule?
Even in famine, the seals held back total destruction, sparing the oil and the wine. Where have you seen God's restraint even in hard seasons, a mercy mixed into judgment?

The sixth seal left every class of person, from kings to slaves, with nowhere to hide except in terror. What would it mean for you to find your hiding place in the Lamb's mercy now, rather than dreading His wrath later?

This chapter has been read by faithful Christians as describing the first century, all of church history, a future tribulation, or the ongoing pattern of this present age. How does holding these different views with humility, while still trusting the chapter's central truth, shape the way you read difficult or uncertain parts of Scripture?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote explains the Greek literally reads 'a choenix of wheat for a denarius,' a choenix being a Greek dry measure of about 1.92 pints, and a denarius customarily a laborer's full day's wage, underscoring just how severe the pictured scarcity is.

The cry 'fall on us and hide us' echoes Hosea 10:8 almost word for word, rooting this scene of terror in an already ancient prophetic warning of judgment on unfaithful Israel, now applied to the whole unrepentant earth.

Some manuscript traditions, including the Byzantine text and the Textus Receptus, read 'His wrath' rather than 'Their wrath' in verse 17; the plural reading ties the wrath jointly to both the One on the throne and the Lamb named in verse 16.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the repo's makor-scripture tool for Revelation 6:1-17. No em or en dashes present in the source verses or used anywhere in this file.
